nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams, shaded forest waterways, and lowland wetlands on the island of Sri Lanka.

summary: Cryptocoryne Undulata is a Sri Lankan aquatic plant valued for its narrow, wavy leaves that take on reddish-brown tones under moderate to strong light. It is generally considered one of the more forgiving Cryptocorynes, tolerating a wide range of water conditions.
careNotes: Cryptocoryne Undulata is generally considered one of the more forgiving members of the Cryptocoryne genus, making it a reasonable first choice for beginner aquascapers. Like other Cryptocorynes, it is prone to what hobbyists call 'crypt melt' — a stress response in which the existing leaves dissolve after relocation or a sudden shift in water parameters. This looks alarming but is not necessarily fatal: the rhizome (the thick horizontal stem that anchors the plant) typically survives and sends up new growth within a few weeks if left undisturbed. Several older scientific names are still encountered in the aquarium trade, including Cryptocoryne axelrodii and Cryptocoryne willisii var. undulata; some current taxonomic treatments also classify this species under Cryptocoryne crispatula var. undulata. A triploid cultivar sold under the 'Broad Leaf' name belongs to the same species. Mature height has been reported anywhere from about 15 cm to over 30 cm, with taller growth generally associated with stronger light, added CO2, and a nutrient-rich substrate.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Designated Buce Plant secondary was swapped — Buce Plant catalog did not surface a Cryptocoryne undulata product page. Fish Laboratory used to cross-check temperature (22-28°C), pH (5.5-7.5), hardness (1-15 dGH), family (Araceae), and historical synonyms (C. axelrodii, C. willisii var. undulata).

additional.name: Aquatics Pool Spa
additional.notes: Third source used to confirm temperature 68-83°F (20-28°C), pH 6.5-7.0, low-to-moderate light, CO2 not required, propagation via runners. Disagrees with FishLab on growth rate (calls it 'rather quick' vs. FishLab's slow); averaged to medium.
